metadata.title: Will She Win? Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries

abstract: In the 2020 Democratic Presidential Primary campaigns, six women, the most in history, announced their candidacy for the presidency. Using computational topic modeling and network analysis of 84,000 newspaper articles (2.2 million paragraphs), we analyze how gender and electability was discussed during the Primaries. Results reveal that male candidates, and especially Joe Biden, were frequently compared with the incumbent, Donald Trump, while coverage of female candidates focused directly on social policy. Trump's attack on Biden during the impeachment process produced a lasting effect by promoting discourse topics traditionally dominated by men. This crowded out the topics emphasized by women, such as health care and education. Women candidates' press coverage was also more reactionary than men's. Although explicit reference to gender as a discourse topic has decreased, which suggests growing gender parity within the political process, gendered policy salience emerges transitively in comparison with the incumbent. Transitive policy salience emerges as a key explanatory variable that articulates the indirect path media coverage can lead to the marginalization of women candidates.

sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: In 2019, an unprecedented total of six women announced their candidacy for the Democratic presidential nomination. Their primary campaigns occurred at the same time that Speaker of the House, Nancy Pelosi, sealed her status as the most powerful and high-ranking elected woman in American political history (Stolberg, 2019). Given that Hillary Clinton won the Democratic Party's presidential nomination in 2016, and that nearly one-fourth of the seats in Congress are now filled by women (DeSilver, 2018), at an all-time high, we have witnessed a trend toward gender parity in U.S. politics.

In this study, we seek to investigate a potential mediating factor in the success of female candidates being elected to government offices: media coverage. Several studies over the last two decades have found that female political candidates receive gender-biased coverage that may be detrimental to their election success (Bode & Hennings, 2012;Bystrom & Dimitrova, 2014;Bystrom & Hennings, 2013;Heldman et al., 2005;Lavery, 2013;Rohrbach et al., 2020;Wasburn & Wasburn, 2011). To the extent that media outlets frame candidates differently based on their gender (Lavery, 2013), or that female candidates receive relatively negative media coverage (Wasburn & Wasburn, 2011), biases and differences in reporting practices may render female candidates less viable for office, leading to lower poll numbers, donations, and ultimately, support.

Importantly, a contemporary line of research focused on pragmatic bias, which describes the phenomenon where even when people are "ready for someone from a historically disadvantaged group to play a leadership role," they may not act because they believe others won't support someone from that disadvantaged group (Corbett et al., 2022). Pragmatic bias can exert a crucial slowing force on social change, and the media can play an important role in exacerbating it based on its framing choices (Willer et al., 2020).

Since the 1992 elections, heralded as the "Year of the Woman" (Smith-Howell, 1995), political communication scholars have attempted to measure the parity and objectivity of media coverage of female candidates (Smith, 1997). While several of these studies demonstrate progress toward gender equity in quantity and substance, gender-differentiated reporting is still present in media today. Recent literature shows that contextual differences moderate the impact of gender, and generate media bias, coverage, and online engagement (Bobba et al., 2018;Rohrbach et al., 2020;Wagner et al., 2017).

The 2020 presidential primary race offers a unique moment to assess gendered factors latent in candidate media coverage. As previously mentioned, six women announced their candidacy for the Democratic nomination, three of whom finished in the top 10. Nancy Pelosi (alongside chair of the House Intelligence Committee, Adam Schiff) was also the face of the Democratic Party's impeachment of Donald Trump. This gave the country a chance to witness the highest-ranking woman in American politics take a steadfast stand against the sitting president. This election is also peculiar in terms of the candidates' comparison with the incumbent, Donald Trump. The ability to "beat Trump" was a frame employed by all the Democratic candidates, which appeals to an uncharacteristically urgent need to replace the sitting president. As Bendle (2014) points out, a voter's perceived degree of electability for a candidate may be influenced not just in comparison with their opponents in their own party, but also against the incumbent. Electability is always referential.

To trace the media coverage of presidential candidates in the 2020 election and its gender-based variance, this study offers a computer-assisted content analysis of newspaper articles from the top U.S. news sources. Using natural language processing, topic modeling, and sentiment analysis, we analyzed 84,000 newspaper articles (2.2 million paragraphs) retrieved from Media Cloud. Upon reporting the general trends across all candidates, we focus on six candidates (three top male and three top female candidates) and the incumbent, Donald Trump, against a backdrop of primary events.

We identify three main takeaways: First, while the absence of explicit genderrelated discourse suggests increased gender parity, we find latent gender bias in the form of gender-stereotyped issues. Of these issues, the most important are topics that were amplified by the incumbent, Donald Trump. Second, we find that candidates whose issues associate with discourse related to Donald Trump tend to be covered significantly more than those that did not. In these primary campaigns, impeachment emerged as a critical agenda-setter, allowing male-dominated discourse topics to flourish, which crowded out women candidates and contributed to the suspension of their campaigns. We define this phenomenon as transitive policy salience-where the media's fixation on a specific opponent sets the policy agenda. Finally, we find the discrepancy between polling and press coverage fluctuates three times as much for women as men, suggesting an acutely reactive response in media content related to campaign strategies and press coverage. Combined with pragmatic bias, this marginalized women through not just across-gender comparisons, but dilution of attention through within-gender comparisons.

Pragmatic bias can exert a crucial slowing force on social change, and the media can play an important role in exacerbating it based on its framing choices (Willer et al., 2020).

Since the 1992 elections, heralded as the "Year of the Woman" (Smith-Howell, 1995), political communication scholars have attempted to measure the parity and objectivity of media coverage of female candidates (Smith, 1997). While several of these studies demonstrate progress toward gender equity in quantity and substance, gender-differentiated reporting is still present in media today. Finally, we find the discrepancy between polling and press coverage fluctuates three times as much for women as men, suggesting an acutely reactive response in media content related to campaign strategies and press coverage. sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: Over the last three decades, the literature on gender differences in the coverage of political candidates has demonstrated a trend toward gender parity, albeit one riddled with inconsistencies between levels of political contest and particular candidates. To start, Kahn and Goldenberg (1991) found that female U.S. Senate candidates received less coverage, more negative coverage, and more viability coverage-coverage discussing issues such as their electability-than did male candidates who received more coverage on their policy positions. The authors argued these disparities in coverage may set up additional constraints for women's electoral success. On the other hand, they discovered that female journalists were more likely to positively highlight "feminine" issues (discussed below) and traits, which may help bolster the chances for women's electoral success. In a follow-up experimental study, mixed results were produced: Gender-differentiated coverage led participants to evaluate male incumbents as "more viable, . . . stronger leader [s], and better able to deal with military issues," while female incumbents were evaluated higher for compassion and ability to handle health care issues (Kahn, 1992). In yet another study, Kahn (1993) investigated the gender differences in advertisement campaigns of U.S. Senate candidates and found that men focused more on economic issues, while women focused more on social issues. Despite the appeals that candidates strategically employed in their ads, which either leveraged gender stereotypes or contested them, news coverage failed to reflect the latter efforts and generally reported on candidates in gender-stereotypical terms.

To summarize, early research on gender and media coverage found that when differences do emerge between male and female candidates, they tend to differ in the amount of coverage, candidate associations with gender-stereotyped policy issues, discussions of viability, coverage of personality or novelty, and gendered campaign strategies. In this section, we elaborate on the literature concerning each potential variable in the news coverage.

sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: The amount of coverage female candidates received compared with men in U.S. electoral politics tends to vary. In the 2000 governor and senate races, women received proportionately more coverage than men did (Bystrom et al., 2001) and, in the subsequent election, nearly equal coverage (Lavery, 2013). Elizabeth Dole-up until that point, the woman with the "longest and most serious" bid for nomination by a woman in a dominant party-received less newspaper coverage than her male opponents did (Heldman et al., 2005). In the subsequent presidential election, the first-ever female vice presidential candidate, Sarah Palin, received 2.3 times more newspaper coverage and seven times more blog coverage than her opponent, Joe Biden, as well as more photo coverage in general (Bode & Hennings, 2012). The greater amount of photo coverage may reflect the commercial appeal of her image, which may have been damaging to her electability. In the 2008 election, Republican presidential candidate Michelle Bachmann received less coverage than her male opponents (Bystrom & Dimitrova, 2014).

Based on the proportions of coverage reported in these studies, no hard conclusions could be drawn about the effects of gender on the amount of coverage. However, the "novelty" of the female candidacy (especially for presidential campaigns)-the more acute focus on women's physical appearance and the particular candidate's perceived electability-can account for the case-to-case variance in coverage.

sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: One aspect of political candidates' media coverage that has grown more equitable over the years, but still tends to surface in electoral rhetoric, is candidate association with "masculine" and "feminine" policy issues. Huddy and Terkildsen (1993) argue that "voters' assumptions about a candidate's gender-linked personality traits drive expectations that women and men have different areas of issue expertise." The issues that tend to be characterized as "feminine" include child care, assisting the poor, solving problems of the elderly, reducing the gender wage gap, abortion, education (Huddy & Terkildsen, 1993), health care, women's rights, the environment, and drug abuse (Lavery, 2013). In contrast, foreign policy, security, and economic issues tend to be constructed as "masculine" issues (Lavery, 2013). Within this policy schema, genderequitable coverage is measured in terms of how each candidate gets covered across these issues in comparison with their counterparts of the opposite gender.

While some extant literature demonstrates only minor discrepancies between the coverage of issues between male and female candidates, others point out significant qualitative and quantitative differences that persist at various levels of political contest. In the 2000 gubernatorial and senate races, women were more likely to be covered on the topic of health care while men were mentioned more on the topics of budget, unemployment, and immigration (Banwart et al., 2003). Lavery (2013) found that television coverage of candidates in the 2002 midterm election offered roughly equitable coverage in terms of policy. In a more localized study-a 2003 gubernatorial race between Kathleen Blanco and Bobby Jindal in Louisiana- Major and Coleman (2008) found that news coverage tended to report more positively on the candidates referencing gender-congruent issues regardless of their experience with gender-incongruent ones, which the authors assert may deepen associations of candidates to gendercorresponding issues.

As for presidential politics, Heldman et al. (2005) found that the 2004 presidential election coverage of candidate Elizabeth Dole was generally equal to that of her male opponents when it came to policy topics. In the subsequent election, Sarah Palin became the second female vice president nominee for a dominant party (after Geraldine Ferraro in 1984). Across two different studies and a variety of media platforms, researchers found that Palin received limited policy coverage compared with her Democratic opponent, Joe Biden, despite receiving more coverage in general (Bode & Hennings, 2012;Wasburn & Wasburn, 2011). Furthermore, when her policies were discussed, the focus tended to be on "women's issues" while Biden's were biased toward "masculine" ones. In the 2012 Senate race, each gender was discussed in relation to opposite gender issues (Bystrom & Hennings, 2013) and Michelle Bachman did not receive more "feminine" issue coverage than her male opponents in the presidential primaries the same year (Bystrom & Dimitrova, 2014).

In contrast, foreign policy, security, and economic issues tend to be constructed as "masculine" issues (Lavery, 2013). Within this policy schema, genderequitable coverage is measured in terms of how each candidate gets covered across these issues in comparison with their counterparts of the opposite gender.

While some extant literature demonstrates only minor discrepancies between the coverage of issues between male and female candidates, others point out significant qualitative and quantitative differences that persist at various levels of political contest. In the 2000 gubernatorial and senate races, women were more likely to be covered on the topic of health care while men were mentioned more on the topics of budget, unemployment, and immigration (Banwart et al., 2003). Lavery (2013) found that television coverage of candidates in the 2002 midterm election offered roughly equitable coverage in terms of policy. In a more localized study-a 2003 gubernatorial race between Kathleen Blanco and Bobby Jindal in Louisiana- Major and Coleman (2008) found that news coverage tended to report more positively on the candidates referencing gender-congruent issues regardless of their experience with gender-incongruent ones, which the authors assert may deepen associations of candidates to gendercorresponding issues.

As for presidential politics, Heldman et al. (2005) found that the 2004 presidential election coverage of candidate Elizabeth Dole was generally equal to that of her male opponents when it came to policy topics. In the subsequent election, Sarah Palin became the second female vice president nominee for a dominant party (after Geraldine Ferraro in 1984). Across two different studies and a variety of media platforms, researchers found that Palin received limited policy coverage compared with her Democratic opponent, Joe Biden, despite receiving more coverage in general (Bode & Hennings, 2012;Wasburn & Wasburn, 2011). Furthermore, when her policies were discussed, the focus tended to be on "women's issues" while Biden's were biased toward "masculine" ones. In the 2012 Senate race, each gender was discussed in relation to opposite gender issues (Bystrom & Hennings, 2013) and Michelle Bachman did not receive more "feminine" issue coverage than her male opponents in the presidential primaries the same year (Bystrom & Dimitrova, 2014).

paragraphs: Previous literature has drawn a distinction between viability, which refers to the likelihood of the candidate receiving the presidential nomination, and electability, which refers to the likelihood of the nominated candidate winning the general election (Abramowitz, 1989). In Abromowitz's (1989) electability model, the perceived viability of a candidate affects the degree to which they are perceived electable by a potential voter. In a more recent model, Bendle (2014) uses electability to account for both viability and electability as both are previously defined. This model accounts for electability by referencing a candidate in relation to other opponents within their party's primary as well as the incumbent in the opposing party.

As previously mentioned, the 2020 election is unique in that all of the Democratic primary candidates who got media coverage appealed to their ability to beat the incumbent, Donald Trump. The general assumption underlying this operationalization is that "[k]nowledge of the opposition in the general election is critical to determining electability," and therefore, a candidate's electability will vary depending on who the candidate is discussed in reference to. In terms of media coverage, electability of a candidate tends to be discussed in comparison with his or her opponent(s), and therefore multiple candidates are likely to be mentioned (Bode & Hennings, 2012). Coverage of this sort is conceptualized as the "horse race" frame, in which the media constructs around candidates to assess their electability and determine who deserves coverage. This frame also justifies why a candidate might not deserve coverage (Lawrence & Rose, 2011). Such coverage tends to focus on personal traits and candidate characteristics, regardless of gender (Lavery, 2013). Furthermore, such frames are typically focused on the candidates' likelihood of winning at the expense of issue-based coverage (Bystrom & Dimitrova, 2014). More recently, research has also focused on the in-group and out-group dynamics of coveragewhere they found female journalists cover women's political qualifications (Bauer, 2022). In terms of visual coverage, recent studies find women are frequently portrayed as less emotionally composed than men. This suggests the portrayals of women candidates may shift depending on modality, thus impacting framings of electability (Gruszczynski et al., 2022), and may be further exacerbated by the growing role of visual-based social media platforms in political processes (Chang et al., 2022).
For this study, we define electability as the likelihood of the candidates' ability to win the general election, which assumes success in the primary.

sections.header_raw: Gendered Campaign Strategy: Policy Versus Personality
sections.header_norm: gendered campaign strategy policy versus personality
paragraphs: Gender stereotypes are well recognized by political candidates and their strategists, driving women candidates to strategically reinforce their association with "feminine" issues or emphasizing their acumen in tackling traditionally "masculine" issues. Candidates leverage or challenge gender stereotypes by emphasizing their issue priorities (Kahn, 1993) and generally prefer speaking to issues in which their party has perceived expertise in addressing (Petrocik, 1996). Windett (2014) also suggests that women are presumably more capable of securing a strong base of support from female voters, as a result of gender affinity, and strategically benefit from gearing their appeals toward male voters. Strategically, the author argues, there is a tendency for women candidates to avoid associating themselves too closely with feminine issues early on in their campaigns in an effort to boost their perceived electability. Ultimately, male candidates benefit from issue divergence, that is, keeping gender-stereotypical issues as a focus of their correspondingly gendered candidates. Conversely, female candidates strategically benefit from issue convergence, allowing them to subvert and downplay gendered assumptions about political strengths (Windett, 2014). A candidate's campaign strategy may also notably change over time, adapting to such factors as polling numbers, the shifting campaign strategies of their opponents, and the political sentiments of the audiences they address on a regional basis.

Gender boundaries and their political implications are routinely negotiated between the media and candidates on the campaign trail (Ritchie, 2013). Huddy and Terkildsen (1993) suggest that "voters' assumptions about a candidate's genderlinked personality traits drive expectations that women and men have different areas of issue expertise." More often than not, personality and policy stance is always in tension. Newspaper coverage of presidential candidate Elizabeth Dole focused more on her personality and appearance in comparison with her male opponents (Heldman et al., 2005). Likewise, vice presidential candidate Sarah Palin received significantly more coverage on "trivial" issues ("discussions of her childhood, family, physical appearance and personality") than her rival, Biden (Wasburn & Wasburn, 2011). In that same election, Democratic primary candidate Hillary Clinton was "constructed at the antithesis of appropriate femininity-cold, calculating, emasculating and brutal," which helped construct a more general image of her as a "preposterous" and even "dangerous" candidate (Ritchie, 2013). This characterization of Hillary as coldblooded and bossy is aptly characterized as early as 1998 in Hating Hillary (Campbell, 1998).

In this regard, we propose the notion of transitive policy salience which captures the tension between personality and policy-If one's novelty and personality are sufficiently strong, they can set the agenda against their opponents, which has significant downstream implications for candidates of the opposite gender.

sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: In this study, we investigate how the media covered men and women candidates in the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries. The 2020 Primaries provides a critical comparison with prior elections, as one of the most diverse democratic primaries.

After Elizabeth Warren, the last women candidate, announced the suspension of her campaign, media outlets were at a loss: How did primaries that began with the excitement of diversity end up with two White male candidates competing? Political commentators pinpointed gendered media coverage as a major reason for the loss of gender diversity. For instance, a CNN article headlined "Women were serious candidates for president. What happened?," discussed the disproportionally high standard of electability for female candidates, saying "often, perhaps always . . . female candidates [seem] held to a higher standard when it came to being presidential, electable-and even likeable" (Hunt, 2020). Or as the New York Times asked, "Was It Always Going to Be the Last Men Standing?" citing "the narrative that somehow women are less electable than men" (Lerer, 2020).

Our principal aim is to then articulate how election discourse evolved around the gender of the candidate. This can occur either at the policy level or candidate level, specifically through direct comparisons with the incumbent. By measuring how these factors emerged in the media coverage of the 2020 Democratic Primaries, we hope to both characterize and theorize the media's role in shaping election narratives, and potentially elections themselves.

RQ1: What disparities in policy topic, electability, and incumbent comparisons can be observed between candidates, based on gender? RQ2: What topics (gendered issue topics, electability, and incumbent comparisons) have the highest association with individual contenders? RQ3: What is the relationship between electability discourse and polling results, based on gender?

sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: Media Cloud, developed by the Berkman Klein Center at the Harvard Law School, provides a repository of all news articles published by major news sources. We used the 50 U.S. Top Newspapers collection compiled by Media Cloud based on the report published by the Pew Research Center in 2018. These 50 newspapers include sources from a wide array of political spectrum and include the New York Times, the New York Post, the Washington Post, and the Wall Street Journal. Furthermore, it comprises local newspapers such as the Seattle Times and Boston Globe, and thus was considered appropriate for our analysis that aims to study the larger media landscape of election coverage in the United States. This is to prevent the polarized media environment in the United States from effecting the analysis of gendered coverage if we were to choose only a few sources. A full list is provided in the Supplemental Appendix.

Using the Media Cloud API, we downloaded articles related to each of the Democratic Candidates. Specifically, Media Cloud exports a list of news article URLs given a list of Boolean search terms. Articles were then retrieved using the Newspaper3k package in Python 3. Articles were scraped from December 1, 2018, to March 12, 2020, the week after Super Tuesday, when Elizabeth Warren announced the suspension of her campaign. Basic statistics of the data set are shown in Figure 1.

To ensure the quality of the search, we imposed a series of Boolean conditionals and keywords (such as the term "presidential"). A full list and description of the conditionals can be found in the Supplemental Appendix. For each candidate, a set of articles is produced. Each article is then tagged in multiple ways. First, the journalist and publishing news agency is recorded.

paragraphs: Next, we label paragraphs (or sentences) that contain the candidate and a specific policy issue. Specifically, suppose we have n policy issues. Let X be a string of text and F i denote the filters for a specific category. The text string is either a paragraph or a sentence. As an example, the filter for electability is shown below:

We search over 12 criteria: Trump, Clinton, electability discourse, and 9 policies-the environment, health care, national security, immigration, taxation, law enforcement, the economy, social issues, and education. The tagging process is repeated for all policies. We chose these topics in consultancy with the Comparative Agendas Project which lists 21 primary policy topics in the United States congress (Baumgartner et al., 2019). We selected the few that had the greatest salience (for instance, excluding culture) and chose keywords based on the codebook. For electability, we started with a seed list of "viable," "electable," and "electability," then selected the top 10 co-occurring terms. This same approach was used for expanding the keyword set for the individual topics and is included in the Supplemental Appendix.

These tags are then stored in a vector of size 12, then timestamped by publication date. Note that some topics, such as health care, taxation, law enforcement, and education, are less likely to be isolated as paragraphs. There are likely several contributing factors to this variation, but the complexity of those topics likely compels journalists toward writing full articles rather than one-off paragraphs. Thus, we compare across both article and paragraph levels. Next, we label paragraphs (or sentences) that contain the candidate and a specific policy issue. Specifically, suppose we have n policy issues. Let X be a string of text and F i denote the filters for a specific category. The text string is either a paragraph or a sentence. As an example, the filter for electability is shown below:

We search over 12 criteria: Trump, Clinton, electability discourse, and 9 policies-the environment, health care, national security, immigration, taxation, law enforcement, the economy, social issues, and education. The tagging process is repeated for all policies. We chose these topics in consultancy with the Comparative Agendas Project which lists 21 primary policy topics in the United States congress (Baumgartner et al., 2019). We selected the few that had the greatest salience (for instance, excluding culture) and chose keywords based on the codebook. For electability, we started with a seed list of "viable," "electable," and "electability," then selected the top 10 co-occurring terms. This same approach was used for expanding the keyword set for the individual topics and is included in the Supplemental Appendix.

These tags are then stored in a vector of size 12, then timestamped by publication date. sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: We begin with RQ1, which considers how our topics vary by the gender of the candidates. Figure 2 shows the co-occurrence of policy issues, electability discourse, and individuals (Trump and Clinton) within news articles. We include Clinton to see whether women candidates were similarly compared with the prior election. Note, having confirmed the topic modeling criteria does not skew results to a particular gender, these bar graphs make direct comparisons between men and women, for each topic. As articles were scraped by Media Cloud, we can treat this as the population level.

In the articles (Figure 2A), we observe most discourse topics being mentioned in relatively equal proportions. The same holds for the number of paragraphs, save cooccurrences with Trump. Male candidates are mentioned almost 4% more in conjunction with Trump, which is around 60,000 paragraphs. This is significant. Although women are mentioned in parity with men, for every article, they are under-mentioned at the paragraph level. Reuning and Dietrich (2019) contend that media coverage, defined as "the extent to which a candidate is covered in the news," influences public interest and may act as a mode of "status conferral" for the candidates, offering voters cues as to who has the best chance of winning. However, it remains unclear the degree to which mentions of candidates may affect their public interest because of where they appear in text (i.e., in the first few paragraphs), how they are discussed in each instance, and which articles are the most read.

Note, the explicit mention of gender issues-men and women, male and femalewas almost non-existent (less than 10 articles total). This may be a sign of progress toward gender equality, or in any case a marked move away from explicit mention of gender as a casually addressed aspect of a particular candidate's viability or comparison with another candidate.

Furthermore, we conducted statistical tests to see whether or not such differences are statistically significant. As the data showed a distribution with severe right skew means, we conducted the Mann-Whitney U test instead of t tests which assume a normal distribution. The results are shown in Figure 3, which demonstrates the distribution of data for men and women for Trump. The test results are summarized in Table 1. The results show that as we predicted from Figure 2, male candidates and female candidates showed a significantly different distribution for the issues relating to Trump, Education, National Security, Economy, Environment, and Social Issues. For issues such as Health Care, Clinton, Electability, Immigration, Law Enforcement, and Taxation, there was no statistically meaningful disparity between candidate gender.

Before we analyze why significant distributions appeared in reference to some topics and not others, we first consider additional differences between the men and women, by conducting a time series analysis. Figure 4 shows the relative proportion of paragraphs among six select discourse topics: Trump, health care, education, the economy, immigration, and national security.

Immediately, we observe a large spike in Trump-related discourse with men in September 2019. This continues throughout the primary season, peaking on Super Tuesday. This likely points to a spike in articles backfilling with explanations of how the impeachment effort developed, especially in discussions between Biden and Trump and Biden and Sanders, and results in a vertical translation within the graph. For other discourse topics, men and women candidates appear highly coupled. However, there are a few exceptions. Women have more coverage in mentioning education, health care, and the economy. In particular, the discourse on education and health care seems to be driven by women during the debates.

We can now answer RQ1. We find there is some alignment of feminine topics with the top women candidates. However, of more significant interest is co-occurrence with Trump, which is significantly overtaken by men and associated with the masculine issues around national security and immigration.

paragraphs: To further delve into why certain topics emerge as more salient, we now turn our attention to RQ2: What topics (gendered issue topics, electability, and incumbent comparisons) have the highest association with individual contenders? We further separate these comparisons into the top 3 men and women candidates, showing precisely who the actors are in Figure 5. Here, the absolute number of articles is shown, with the top 3 men (Biden, Sanders, Buttigieg) and top 3 women (Warren, Harris, Klobuchar). The story becomes clear here. The co-occurrence with Trump is tied heavily with Joe Biden, including the discourse regarding National Security. Education was initially driven by Elizabeth Warren, then overtaken by Sanders. Interestingly, Kamala Harris is one of the first to focus on health care and preserves her top spot against traditional candidates. This may be attributed to her shifting position on health care (Laszewski, 2020). Naturally, we also observe a decrease in coverage after Harris drops out. Of course, this data does not exist in a vacuum.

To address what appears to be the most drastic shifts in discourse, we provide some event-based relationships for consideration. Looking to the "Trump" segment of Figure 2, a visible, disproportionate spike of male discourse surrounds the September to October 2019 timeframe-This is almost entirely due to a series of articles discussing Trump's references to Joe Biden via Hunter Biden's Ukrainian connections, such as Washington Post's September piece titled "Trump was fixated on attacking Biden long before his call with Ukraine." Figure 3 is similarly explicable. "National Security" topic discussion rose noticeably for candidate Biden during the impeachment hearings, likely due to addresses by President Trump alleging some misconduct and resulting national insecurity.

The disconnect between male and female candidates in this graphic reduces significantly as the impeachment hearings return to center stage and candidates' response to those proceedings is made public. In addition, the two primary topic sectors where major spikes were driven by female candidates, "Health Care" and "Education," centered on candidates Warren and Harris's policy addresses on those topics. Note as well that when isolated by the candidate the role of candidates Warren and Sanders in driving the "Health Care" and "Education" topics becomes apparent, especially around the middle and then end of 2019. Both occurrences seem to be tied to instances where one or both candidates explicitly targeted Biden (Warren) or Warren and Biden (Berman, 2019) on those topics, especially the shortcomings of Biden's proposed Affordable Care Act amendments and student lending record.

Part of the limited success of female candidates may be attributed to these discourse topics. We observe how the main discourse topics of female candidates-education and health care-are not associated with topics (national security) more salient to Trump. To make this precise, Figure 6 shows the cluster correlation maps of the discourse time series, with the colors representing the time-series correlation between discourse topics. Correlation maps are standard visualizations for understanding how closely related topics are to each other. Red indicates two topics were mentioned together; blue indicates less frequently. Clustering adds an additional layer of hierarchical information, by ensuring multiple issues that have high correlations are plotted together.

What we observe is that health care and education have a much lower correlation with electability and Trump, indicated in blue, possibly due to their complexity and political sensitivity encouraging more dedicated coverage. Issues such as immigration, national security, and the economy have a much higher correlation. Previous research has demonstrated the former topics tend to be stereotyped as "feminine" issues and the latter as "masculine" issues (Heldman et al., 2005;Huddy & Terkildsen, 1993;Lavery, 2013). While previous literature has argued that such stereotyping can be advantageous for female candidates, who are perceived as more adept at handling these issues (Bystrom & Dimitrova, 2014;Kahn, 1992), our research suggests that these issues were marginalized and therefore negatively impacted the perceived electability of the candidates who associated with them. Furthermore, we contend that policy issues that correlate with electability and Trump will perform better. The corollary here is that women candidates disproportionally mention issues around social policy, particularly Warren and Harris.

We investigate the pairwise sentiment between candidates. To do this, we consider the networks of candidate co-occurrences. Figure 7 shows the networks of portrayals across candidates during different time frames. We first separated the articles into three time periods-before September 2019, between September 1, 2019 and February 8, 2020 (the eighth debate), and February 8, 2020 until March 12, 2020. We observe a few trends. First, the strengths of the connections shift from the minor, nonconventional candidates such as Harris, Gabbard, and Yang, to the center and newcomers, such as Bloomberg. More importantly, the Washington Post-the outlet with the most articles-disproportionately published articles that link Biden and Trump within this central period. Two likely explanations may be traced here. First, Biden has significant institutional connections with the Washington Post, a newspaper located in the national capital and deeply interested in national establishment politics, and as such comparison between the establishment frontrunner and incumbent may be anticipated. Second, looking beyond the Washington Post, the discussion between the two is significant across several of the outlets during different time phases. Some of this is likely due to Biden's role as Obama's heir apparent, but it is also likely that Biden and those supporting his nomination understand that comparison with Trump is the strongest possible argument for the former vice president's nomination. Focusing on defeating Trump allows Biden to more easily deflect from talking about policy.

To summarize, with the media highlighting the impeachment process, Biden not only received the most amount of media coverage, he was also at the center of the most highlighted issues during the Democratic presidential primaries, that being which candidate had the best odds of defeating Trump. Given that the media devoted significant coverage to the impeachment hearings rather than focusing on the specific policy proposals put forward by the candidates, the female candidates were in effect marginalized in the media discourse. A Washington Post opinion piece titled The No.1 reason Biden is likely to beat Trump is a good example of the dominant media framing that helped Joe Biden become the nominee. Boot (2020) explained Joe Biden as the preferred choice because "he doesn't have a radical agenda. He isn't a new face; he has been involved in national politics longer than the median American has been alive," and argues that "ultimately, I suspect, the outcome can be ascribed to the simple fact that most people like "Uncle Joe." In other words, the most mainstream and centrist candidate Joe Biden could subvert the notorious novelty of Trump's administration, whereas women candidates associated with new novelty could not.

Both occurrences seem to be tied to instances where one or both candidates explicitly targeted Biden (Warren) or Warren and Biden (Berman, 2019) on those topics, especially the shortcomings of Biden's proposed Affordable Care Act amendments and student lending record.

Part of the limited success of female candidates may be attributed to these discourse topics. We observe how the main discourse topics of female candidates-education and health care-are not associated with topics (national security) more salient to Trump. To make this precise, Figure 6 shows the cluster correlation maps of the discourse time series, with the colors representing the time-series correlation between discourse topics. Correlation maps are standard visualizations for understanding how closely related topics are to each other. Red indicates two topics were mentioned together; blue indicates less frequently. Clustering adds an additional layer of hierarchical information, by ensuring multiple issues that have high correlations are plotted together.

What we observe is that health care and education have a much lower correlation with electability and Trump, indicated in blue, possibly due to their complexity and political sensitivity encouraging more dedicated coverage. Issues such as immigration, national security, and the economy have a much higher correlation. Previous research has demonstrated the former topics tend to be stereotyped as "feminine" issues and the latter as "masculine" issues (Heldman et al., 2005;Huddy & Terkildsen, 1993;Lavery, 2013). While previous literature has argued that such stereotyping can be advantageous for female candidates, who are perceived as more adept at handling these issues (Bystrom & Dimitrova, 2014;Kahn, 1992), our research suggests that these issues were marginalized and therefore negatively impacted the perceived electability of the candidates who associated with them. Furthermore, we contend that policy issues that correlate with electability and Trump will perform better. The corollary here is that women candidates disproportionally mention issues around social policy, particularly Warren and Harris.

We investigate the pairwise sentiment between candidates. To do this, we consider the networks of candidate co-occurrences. Figure 7 shows the networks of portrayals across candidates during different time frames. We first separated the articles into three time periods-before September 2019, between September 1, 2019 and February 8, 2020 (the eighth debate), and February 8, 2020 until March 12, 2020. We observe a few trends. First, the strengths of the connections shift from the minor, nonconventional candidates such as Harris, Gabbard, and Yang, to the center and newcomers, such as Bloomberg. More importantly, the Washington Post-the outlet with the most articles-disproportionately published articles that link Biden and Trump within this central period. Two likely explanations may be traced here. First, Biden has significant institutional connections with the Washington Post, a newspaper located in the national capital and deeply interested in national establishment politics, and as such comparison between the establishment frontrunner and incumbent may be anticipated. Second, looking beyond the Washington Post, the discussion between the two is significant across several of the outlets during different time phases. Some of this is likely due to Biden's role as Obama's heir apparent, but it is also likely that Biden and those supporting his nomination understand that comparison with Trump is the strongest possible argument for the former vice president's nomination. Focusing on defeating Trump allows Biden to more easily deflect from talking about policy.

To summarize, with the media highlighting the impeachment process, Biden not only received the most amount of media coverage, he was also at the center of the most highlighted issues during the Democratic presidential primaries, that being which candidate had the best odds of defeating Trump. Given that the media devoted significant coverage to the impeachment hearings rather than focusing on the specific policy proposals put forward by the candidates, the female candidates were in effect marginalized in the media discourse. A Washington Post opinion piece titled The No.1 reason Biden is likely to beat Trump is a good example of the dominant media framing that helped Joe Biden become the nominee. Boot (2020) explained Joe Biden as the preferred choice because "he doesn't have a radical agenda. He isn't a new face; he has been involved in national politics longer than the median American has been alive," and argues that "ultimately, I suspect, the outcome can be ascribed to the simple fact that most people like "Uncle Joe." In other words, the most mainstream and centrist candidate Joe Biden could subvert the notorious novelty of Trump's administration, whereas women candidates associated with new novelty could not.
sections.preview_head: To further delve into why certain topics emerge as more salient, we now turn our attention to RQ2: What topics (gendered issue topics, electability, and incumbent comparisons) have the highest association with individual contenders? We further separate these comparisons into the top 3 men and women candidates, showing precisely who the actors are in Figure 5. Here, the absolute number of articles is shown, with the top 3 men (Biden, Sanders, Buttigieg) and top 3 women (Warren, Harris, Klobuchar).

sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: We have now shown that which topics-across policy, electability, and candidate comparisons-intersect most closely by gender, how these topics relate to one another, and which candidates seem to benefit the most in framing. Finally, we consider electability directly in relation to polling results, as part of RQ3. Figure 8A shows the percentage of articles that cover women and men in regard to electability, overtime. We see quite clearly that at no point were the number of articles that discussed the electability of women greater than that of men. Note, these percentages do not sum to one as candidates can be mentioned in tandem. Figure 8B shows the polling results over time and shows men were polled to be much more likely over the entire primaries.

What is more useful in our context is the discrepancy between the press and polls. Figure 8C and D shows the standardized difference for women and men, respectively. A standardized difference of 0 indicates the press accurately represents polling numbers in amount of coverage; a value greater than 0 means overrepresentation and less than 0 means less representation.

We find that the electability coverage fluctuates with polling three times as much for women than men, shown in yellow. Causality is impossible to pin, but as in most social systems, these are likely feedback loops. As such, we evaluate both directions. The first possibility is that polling results impact electability discourse, specifically by reinscribing "pragmatic biases" toward women candidates. Pragmatic bias occurs when "when people withhold support for a member of a group because they see success to be difficult or impossible for members of that group to achieve" (Corbett et al., 2022). This bias qualitatively differs from the personal biases, toward women as a group, that affect more abstract notions of ability to address issues rather than their sheer ability to win in political contests. In sum, the quantity of electability discourse for women candidates may fluctuate significantly in either direction based on their electability as indicated by poll numbers. This can arise from both journalistic coverage or the candidate's campaign themselves. Inversely, the amount of electability discourse in the media may shape poll numbers. In this regard, there is wide literature showing how news coverage of campaigns influences voters one way or another, broadly and specific to gender (Druckman, 2005).

In either case, we show that either news coverage for women appear to be more reactive than men to polling numbers, or voters respond stronger to how women appear in the press. Whether the change in news coverage is driven by the candidates themselves or the press remains an open question. What we can infer is that, because of the masculine centric topics set by Trump, women candidates were constantly on a backfoot adjusting their communication and strategy.

sections.paper_id: Chang et al. - 2024 - Will She Win Gendered Media Coverage of the 2020 Democratic Party Presidential Primaries
paragraphs: We began this article investigating whether or not there was a difference in issue coverage based on the gender of candidates, due to the incredible Democratic showing in 2020. While we do find differences, the explanation is more complex than we initially hypothesized. The most significant divergences were with Trump, visually, statistically, and temporally (Figures 234). Health Care or Education, where women candidates such as Elizabeth Warren or Kamala Harris were leading, did not make it to the center of the primary discourse. Topics that cluster heavily together with Trump were typically masculine, and the negative sentiment between Trump and Biden pre-emptively framed them as "the opposition." Finally, women candidates react almost three times as much as do men in polling (standardized difference of 9.992 versus 3.597), which suggests a more reactionary role in campaign strategy or journalistic overreaction, and an uphill, reactionary approach to setting policy agendas.

In other words, issues that do have statistical significance are all associated with the rhetoric of Donald Trump. This gives us an alternative explanatory pathway, where comparisons are first at the individual level, then propagate to the gendered policy issues. The gendering effect of the policy topic is conditional on the incumbent, although there are many issues that do converge for women and men candidates. What is interesting are topics that do not converge.

Before discussing downstream implications, it is also necessary to review possible alternative explanations for fluctuations in coverage. Both Biden and Sanders were Note. This shows electability coverage fluctuates with polling three times as much for women than men. covered as a person to "beat Trump" due to their experience and recognition, rather than gender. However, we found there were no disparities in media coverage in many policy issues. As such, if political experience and campaign robustness were decisive factors in coverage, this would have been noticable in issue coverage as well. However, the gaps that emerged were the ones correlated with Trump. In other words, it is the intersection of policy issues, incumbent comparisons, and viability that dictate media coverage.

We thus offer three broad contributions. First, we expand on the theory of gendered issue coverage that differences in the supply of journalistic coverage are determined by individual comparisons to the incumbent, which may propagate into gendered issue coverage. We offer a new explanatory pathway with the concept of transitive policy salience (TPS) where a strong focus on a candidate's character, including their heterodox personality and political ideas, shapes the relational coverage of candidates of the opposite gender. Many of the issues provided in the literature only address individual variables at the macro-level, and rarely do studies consider the interaction of the incumbent, viability discourse, and tone with individual policy topics (Van der Pas & Aaldering, 2020).

Second, TPS explicitly reveals the pathway in which media framing exacerbates pragmatic bias. As we showed in the literature review, pragmatic bias may cause people to find it difficult to vote for women even if they appear qualified, because they perceive them as traditionally less viable (Corbett et al., 2022). If journalistic coverage has any influence over political decision-making, then the way women candidates are compared with men matter. Beyond cross-gender comparisons, prior research has also shown gender shifting may be harder when there are more women candidates. Our research found feminine topics distributed across different candidates. As such, our results would suggest less is better-if direct comparisons with the incumbent are critical, and switching cost lower with fewer women, then concentrating comparisons with the right candidate may improve the success rate of a woman candidate. It would then be important for the media to not diffuse issue-based comparisons.

Finally, in terms of methodology, our research also reveals the difficulty of measuring gender bias, since the incumbent may choose to engage with a select few or none of the specific policies (Van der Pas & Aaldering, 2020). In other words, gendered biases are methodologically hard to measure due to agenda-setting by the incumbent. It also explains why there are contradictory findings regarding gender and the visibility of politicians. TPS thus addresses this significant gap left in Van der Pas and Aaldering's (2020) study, which observes we have only limited insight into the nature of any gender bias in the media coverage of politicians . . . usually finding differences in the coverage of men and women politicians in at least one, but not all, of the studied aspects (p.115).

TPS provides an explanation of why a subset of policy issues become significant. We begin this line of investigation here, by conditioning gendered issue salience upon the incumbent through both time series and network analysis. More robust correlational measures can be developed, by computing the conditional synergy of candidate and topic (Chang et al., 2021a(Chang et al., , 2021b)).

One limitation of our work is distinguishing effects from journalistic framing versus strategic pivots from campaign strategies. While news analysis at this scale is powerful in understanding candidate portrayals, the source of these changes remains a mystery. Qualitative analysis of campaigns overtime may bridge this gap-for instance, each candidate appears in different states at different times and they will inevitably change their strategy based on the region they are addressing. Second, some way of measuring distance between official campaign press releases and portrayals in the media would allow a metric of a discrepancy, and improve our understanding of TPS.

In sum, gendered policy discourse is produced through transitive policy salience, transferring via comparison from the incumbent. This produces two important downstream implications. Across gender, media framing may inadvertently exacerbate pragmatic bias toward woman candidates in general. Within gender and in a pool of many women candidates, TPS may dilute the viability of individual women candidates, by increasing the shifting cost. These two forces of in-gender and out-gender comparisons ultimately diminish the viability of women candidates. Moving forward, it will be valuable to observe how the Democrat candidates respond to Biden as the incumbent, when the protest vote is no longer a return to normalcy but one of a progressive future.
